blocks.content: By noon, Ethan had set up his small travel easel near the edge of the park.
He had not followed her there.
He told himself that three times.
He had planned to paint the park eventually. Probably. Maybe. The light was good, and the archway had a nice shape, and the fountain was broken in an interesting way.
Those were all legitimate artistic reasons.
None of them explained why he chose the bench near the archway as the center of the composition.
Ethan squeezed blue paint onto his palette, then white, then a small amount of green. He worked better when he stopped thinking about whether he was working well.
He let the brush move loose at first, capturing mood before detail.
People passed occasionally. A jogger. An older couple. A woman pushing a stroller. Ethan kept his headphones in with nothing playing, the universal signal for please do not perceive me.
Until a voice behind him said,

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That fountain looks better in your painting than it does in real life.
Ethan startled so hard he nearly dragged a line of blue across the canvas.
His brain, normally unreliable in social settings, decided this was the perfect moment to resign completely.

She leaned slightly to look at the canvas, eyes bright with curiosity. Up close, she was even more striking, but not in the polished way he had expected. There was a small freckle near her left cheekbone. A faint smudge of ink on one finger. Wind had pulled loose strands of hair across her face, and she did not seem interested in fixing them.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I didn't mean to scare you.
His voice came out too fast.
She glanced at the brush in his hand, then at the tiny blue mark now streaked across his knuckle.
There it was again. The thrown match.
Ethan stared for half a second too long before remembering names were usually exchanged in these situations.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That sounded less dumb in my head.
